1:
"Paul, a servant of Jesus Christ, called to be an apostle, separated unto the gospel of God," The witnesses note Paul's three-fold self-designation. On "servant" (doulos), the grammatical voices note the force. Robertson and others note doulos means bondservant or slave — Paul glories in being wholly owned by Christ, his Lord and Master. The lowliness is also dignity: a servant of so great a Lord.

On "called to be an apostle," the witnesses note the office. Paul was an apostle not by his own choosing but by divine call (Galatians 1:1) — his authority derived from Christ, not man.

On "separated unto the gospel of God," the witnesses note the consecration. The eastern Chrysostom and Theodoret note Paul was set apart (aphorizo) for the gospel — as at his conversion and commission (Acts 9:15; 13:2). Calvin notes the gospel is "of God" — its author God himself. The verse displays Paul's threefold designation: servant, apostle, separated.

The witnesses agree: Paul opens with a threefold self-designation — a servant (doulos, bondservant, wholly owned by Christ, Robertson), called to be an apostle (by divine call, not man, Galatians 1:1), separated (set apart, aphorizo) unto the gospel of God (its author God himself, Calvin; as at his commission, Acts 9:15; 13:2, Chrysostom, Theodoret). The verse displays Paul's threefold designation. The verse marks the apostle's humility and authority: servant yet apostle. The verse displays the divine call: Paul an apostle by God's appointment. The verse displays the consecration: separated unto the gospel. The verse displays the lowliness and dignity: a servant of so great a Lord. The threefold designation — servant, apostle, separated — displays both Paul's humility and his divine commission. The verse displays the source of his authority: Christ, not man. The verse displays the gospel as God's own: its author and origin divine. The verse marks the opening of the epistle: Paul establishing his credentials to address the Roman church. The verse displays the apostle's identity wholly bound up with Christ and the gospel: a servant owned by Christ, an apostle called by God, a man set apart for the gospel. The threefold self-designation displays Paul's humble yet authoritative standing as the servant and apostle of Jesus Christ, separated unto the gospel of God, which he now proceeds to unfold to the Romans.
2:
"(Which he had promised afore by his prophets in the holy scriptures,)" The witnesses note the gospel's ancient promise. On "promised afore by his prophets," the grammatical voices note the continuity. Barnes, Robertson, and Calvin note the gospel was no novelty but the fulfillment of what God promised long before through the prophets in the Scriptures — the Old Testament foretelling the gospel.

On "in the holy scriptures," the witnesses note the divine authority. The eastern Theodoret and others note the gospel rests on the holy Scriptures, promised by God through his prophets — its roots deep in the Old Testament.

The witnesses note the apologetic force: Paul establishes that the gospel is not new but the long-promised fulfillment, binding the Testaments together. The verse displays the gospel's ancient promise in the prophets.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the gospel was promised afore by God through his prophets in the holy Scriptures — no novelty but the fulfillment of the Old Testament promise (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Theodoret). The verse displays the gospel's ancient promise: foretold by the prophets. The verse marks the continuity of the Testaments: the gospel rooted in the Scriptures. The verse displays the apologetic force: the gospel not new but long-promised. The verse displays the divine authority: the gospel resting on the holy Scriptures. The verse displays the unity of revelation: the prophets foretelling the gospel God now proclaims. The ancient promise of the gospel displays the harmony of the Old and New Testaments. The verse displays God's faithfulness: fulfilling what he promised long before. The verse displays the gospel's pedigree: ancient, promised, scriptural. The verse marks Paul's establishment of the gospel's continuity with the prophets, binding the Testaments together. The ancient promise of the gospel, foretold by the prophets in the holy Scriptures, displays the gospel as the fulfillment of God's long-standing purpose, no novelty but the realization of the Old Testament hope. The verse displays the deep scriptural roots of the gospel Paul preached, promised by God through his prophets and now made manifest in Christ. The verse sets up the description of the gospel's content: God's Son (vv.3-4).
3:
"Concerning his Son Jesus Christ our Lord, which was made of the seed of David according to the flesh;" The witnesses note the gospel's subject and the Lord's human nature. On "concerning his Son," the grammatical voices note the heart of the gospel. Calvin notes the whole gospel is included in Christ: he who removes one step from Christ withdraws from the gospel. The gospel's subject is God's Son.

On "of the seed of David according to the flesh," the witnesses note the human nature. Barnes and Robertson note "according to the flesh" implies that Christ had another nature besides the human — for the phrase is used of no mere man. He was descended from David in his human nature, fulfilling the Messianic promise (2 Samuel 7).

The Reformed Calvin notes the two natures: two things must be found in Christ for salvation — divinity and humanity; the divinity possessing power and righteousness, conveyed to us through the humanity. The verse displays Christ the Son, of David's seed according to the flesh.

The witnesses agree: the gospel concerns God's Son, Jesus Christ our Lord, made of the seed of David according to the flesh — the gospel's subject the Son (Calvin: the whole gospel included in Christ), his human nature descended from David (fulfilling the Messianic promise), "according to the flesh" implying another nature besides the human (Barnes, Robertson). Calvin notes the two natures: divinity and humanity both in Christ, the divinity's power conveyed through the humanity. The verse displays Christ the Son, of David's seed according to the flesh. The verse marks the gospel's subject: God's Son. The verse displays the human nature: descended from David. The verse displays the Messianic fulfillment: the seed of David, the promised King. The verse displays the implication of "according to the flesh": a higher nature besides the human. The Son of David's seed according to the flesh displays both Christ's true humanity and the hint of his higher nature (v.4). The verse displays the gospel centered on the person of Christ. The verse displays the fulfillment of the Davidic promise: the Messiah of David's line. The verse displays the two natures: the human (of David) and, implied, the divine (v.4). The Son, of the seed of David according to the flesh, displays the true humanity of Christ and his Messianic descent, the gospel's subject and the fulfillment of the ancient promise. The verse sets up the declaration of his divine Sonship by the resurrection (v.4).
4:
"And declared to be the Son of God with power, according to the spirit of holiness, by the resurrection from the dead:" The witnesses note the declaration of Christ's divine Sonship — and the meaning of "declared." On "declared" (horizo), the grammatical voices debate. Barnes, Robertson, and Calvin note horizo means to mark out, define, determine — the resurrection did not make him the Son but powerfully declared, marked out, and proved him to be what he eternally was. Gill stresses: not made, but manifested.

On "with power... by the resurrection," the witnesses note the proof. Robertson notes the resurrection definitely marked Jesus off as God's Son, giving God's seal "in power." The resurrection vindicated his claims.

On "according to the spirit of holiness," the witnesses note the divine nature. Ellicott and Robertson note this stands in antithesis to "according to the flesh" — denoting his higher, divine nature (or, Calvin/Robertson, his ethical nature in which the divinity resided), not the Holy Spirit precisely. The verse displays Christ declared the Son of God by the resurrection.

The witnesses agree: Christ was declared (horizo, marked out, defined, proved — not made, but manifested, Gill, Barnes, Robertson, Calvin) to be the Son of God with power by the resurrection from the dead, according to the spirit of holiness (his higher nature, in antithesis to "according to the flesh," Ellicott, Robertson). The resurrection definitely marked Jesus off as God's Son, vindicating his claims (Robertson). The verse displays Christ declared the Son of God by the resurrection. The verse marks the meaning of "declared": not made but manifested, proved, marked out. The verse displays the resurrection as the seal: powerfully declaring his Sonship. The verse displays the two natures: the flesh (v.3) and the spirit of holiness. The verse displays the divine vindication: the resurrection proving his claims. The declaration of Christ's divine Sonship by the resurrection displays both his eternal deity (manifested, not conferred) and the resurrection as its proof. The verse displays the antithesis: flesh and spirit, human and divine. The verse displays the cardinal proof: the resurrection sealing his Sonship in power. The verse, with v.3, displays the full dignity of Christ: of David according to the flesh, the Son of God according to the spirit of holiness. The declaration of Christ as the Son of God with power by the resurrection displays the deity of Christ, manifested and proved (not made) by his rising from the dead, the cardinal vindication of his eternal Sonship.
5:
"By whom we have received grace and apostleship, for obedience to the faith among all nations, for his name:" The witnesses note Paul's commission and its purpose. On "grace and apostleship," the grammatical voices note the two gifts. Ellicott notes grace is the favour Paul possessed as a Christian (especially his conversion), apostleship the office peculiar to him; Calvin: gratuitous apostleship, wholly of divine favour, not his own worth.

On "obedience to the faith" (obedience of faith), the witnesses debate. Robertson, Ellicott, and Calvin note the phrase means "the obedience which springs from faith" — faith itself the act of obedient surrender (the chief act of obedience to God is faith, the chief disobedience unbelief, Calvin), rather than obedience to a body of doctrine.

On "among all nations, for his name," the witnesses note the scope and end. Paul's apostleship extended to all the Gentiles (Acts 9:15), and its end was the glory of Christ's name. The verse displays Paul's commission: grace and apostleship for the obedience of faith.

The witnesses agree: Paul received grace and apostleship (grace his favour as a Christian, apostleship his peculiar office, Ellicott; gratuitous, wholly of divine favour, Calvin) for the obedience of faith among all the nations, for Christ's name — "obedience of faith" meaning the obedience which springs from faith, faith itself the obedient surrender (Robertson, Ellicott, Calvin: the chief act of obedience to God is faith). The verse displays Paul's commission and its purpose. The verse marks the two gifts: grace and apostleship. The verse displays the purpose: the obedience of faith among the nations. The verse displays the scope: all the Gentiles. The verse displays the end: Christ's name glorified. The commission of grace and apostleship for the obedience of faith displays both Paul's divine appointment and the goal of his ministry. The verse displays the nature of faith: itself an obedience, a surrender to Christ. The verse displays the universal scope: among all nations. The verse displays the ultimate end: for his name, the glory of Christ. Paul's commission of grace and apostleship for the obedience of faith displays the divine origin and the universal aim of his ministry: to bring all nations to the obedience that springs from faith, for the glory of Christ's name. The verse displays the heart of Paul's calling: faith and obedience joined, the gospel's fruit among the Gentiles, all for the honor of Christ.
6:
"Among whom are ye also the called of Jesus Christ:" The brief verse notes the Romans' inclusion among the called. The grammatical voices note the address. Gill and others note the Romans were among the nations to whom Paul was sent, and now were "the called of Jesus Christ" — called by and belonging to Christ.

On "the called of Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the effectual call. Calvin (on v.6-7) notes the called are not merely those to whom the gospel is addressed, but those effectually called, made partakers of Christ — chosen by the Father, committed to Christ's care.

The eastern Theodoret and others note the Romans, once among the Gentiles in darkness, were now called by Christ — included in the harvest of the nations. The verse displays the Romans among the called of Jesus Christ.

The very brief comment matches the briefest verse. The witnesses agree: the Romans were among the nations to whom Paul was sent, now "the called of Jesus Christ" — called by and belonging to Christ (Gill), the effectual call making them partakers of Christ (Calvin), once in Gentile darkness, now called (Theodoret). The verse displays the Romans among the called. The verse marks their inclusion: among the nations, now called by Christ. The verse displays the effectual call: belonging to Christ. The verse displays the transition: from Gentile darkness to the call of Christ. The verse displays Paul's connection to the Romans: they within his commission to the nations. The Romans among the called of Jesus Christ display the reach of the gospel and the effectual call that made them Christ's own. The verse displays the grace shown them: called out of the nations. The verse displays their standing: belonging to Christ, called by him. The verse marks Paul's link to the Roman church: they among the Gentiles he was commissioned to reach. The Romans, the called of Jesus Christ, display the fruit of the gospel among the nations and the effectual call that brought them, once in darkness, into the possession of Christ. The verse displays the apostle's tender connection with the church he addresses: they within his commission, called and beloved of Christ. The verse sets up the formal greeting (v.7).
7:
"To all that be in Rome, beloved of God, called to be saints: Grace to you and peace from God our Father, and the Lord Jesus Christ." The witnesses note the address and the apostolic greeting. On "beloved of God, called to be saints," the grammatical voices note the Romans' standing. They were beloved of God and called saints (holy ones) — set apart, consecrated to God. Calvin notes the calling flows from God's love.

On "grace... and peace," the witnesses note the salutation. Barnes and Robertson note Paul's characteristic greeting: grace (the source, God's free favour) and peace (the fruit, reconciliation and well-being) — the sum of all blessing.

On "from God our Father, and the Lord Jesus Christ," the witnesses note the joint source. The grace and peace flow from the Father and the Lord Jesus together — a witness to Christ's deity, placed beside the Father as the source of divine blessing. The verse displays the address and apostolic greeting.

The witnesses agree: Paul addresses all in Rome, beloved of God and called to be saints (holy ones, set apart, the calling flowing from God's love, Calvin), with his characteristic greeting — grace (God's free favour, the source) and peace (the fruit, reconciliation, Barnes, Robertson) — from God the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ jointly (a witness to Christ's deity, placed beside the Father). The verse displays the address and apostolic greeting. The verse marks the Romans' standing: beloved, called saints. The verse displays the salutation: grace and peace. The verse displays the joint source: the Father and the Lord Jesus. The verse displays the witness to Christ's deity: Christ beside the Father as the fountain of blessing. The address and greeting display the Romans' privileged standing and the apostolic blessing. The verse displays the order: grace the source, peace the fruit. The verse displays the love of God: the ground of their calling. The verse displays the saints: holy ones, consecrated to God. The address and apostolic greeting display the Romans as beloved, called saints, and the rich blessing of grace and peace flowing from the Father and the Lord Jesus Christ. The verse displays the deity of Christ, joined with the Father as the source of divine grace and peace, and the privileged standing of the believers, beloved of God and called to holiness. The verse closes the salutation and opens the body of the epistle (v.8).
8:
"First, I thank my God through Jesus Christ for you all, that your faith is spoken of throughout the whole world." The witnesses note Paul's thanksgiving for the Romans' faith. On "I thank my God through Jesus Christ," the grammatical voices note the manner. Chrysostom and others note Paul begins with thanksgiving, offered through Christ the Mediator — all our prayers and praises ascending through him.

On "your faith is spoken of throughout the whole world," the witnesses note the renown. The faith of the Roman church, in the capital of the empire, was famed everywhere — a cause of thanksgiving and a testimony to the gospel's power.

The puritan Henry and the eastern Chrysostom note Paul's wisdom and love: beginning with praise of their faith, winning their hearts, and giving God the glory. The verse displays Paul's thanksgiving for the Romans' renowned faith.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul begins with thanksgiving to God through Jesus Christ (the Mediator, through whom all praise ascends, Chrysostom) for the Romans, whose faith was spoken of throughout the whole world — famed everywhere, in the capital of the empire, a testimony to the gospel's power. Henry and Chrysostom note Paul's wisdom and love: beginning with praise, winning their hearts, giving God the glory. The verse displays Paul's thanksgiving for their renowned faith. The verse marks the manner: thanksgiving through Christ. The verse displays the renown: their faith famed everywhere. The verse displays Paul's love: praising their faith. The verse displays the Mediator: praise ascending through Christ. Paul's thanksgiving for the Romans' renowned faith displays both his love for them and his habit of giving God the glory. The verse displays the testimony: the Roman church's faith spoken of throughout the world. The verse displays the apostle's gracious opening: praise before exhortation. The verse displays the centrality of Christ: thanksgiving offered through him. Paul's thanksgiving for the Romans' faith, famed throughout the world, displays his love, his habit of praise, and the renown of the Roman church's faith — a testimony to the gospel's power even in the heart of the empire. The verse displays the apostle's gracious, Christ-centered opening, winning the Romans' hearts before unfolding his great argument. The verse sets up Paul's account of his prayers and longing to visit them (vv.9-15).
9:
"For God is my witness, whom I serve with my spirit in the gospel of his Son, that without ceasing I make mention of you always in my prayers;" The witnesses note Paul's constant prayer for the Romans. On "God is my witness," the grammatical voices note the solemn appeal. Paul calls God to witness the sincerity of his concern — a solemn oath attesting his unceasing prayers.

On "whom I serve with my spirit," the witnesses note the inward worship. Robertson and Calvin note Paul served God not with mere outward ceremony but with his spirit (the whole inward man) in the gospel of his Son — wholehearted, spiritual service.

On "without ceasing I make mention of you," the witnesses note the constancy. Paul prayed for the Romans always, unceasingly — his love expressed in faithful intercession. The verse displays Paul's solemn witness to his constant prayer.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul solemnly calls God to witness that, serving him with his spirit (the whole inward man, not mere ceremony, Robertson, Calvin) in the gospel of his Son, he made unceasing mention of the Romans in his prayers — his love expressed in faithful, constant intercession. The verse displays Paul's solemn witness to his constant prayer. The verse marks the solemn appeal: God his witness. The verse displays the spiritual service: serving God with his spirit. The verse displays the constancy: unceasing prayer. The verse displays the love: faithful intercession for the Romans. Paul's solemn witness to his constant prayer displays both his sincerity and his love for a church he had not yet seen. The verse displays the inward worship: serving God in spirit. The verse displays the apostle's heart: praying always for the Romans. The verse displays the gospel of his Son: the sphere of Paul's service. Paul's solemn witness to his unceasing prayer displays his deep love for the Roman church and his wholehearted, spiritual service of God in the gospel. The verse displays the apostle's faithful intercession, calling God to witness the sincerity of his concern for a church he longed to visit. The verse sets up his expressed desire to come to them (v.10).
10:
"Making request, if by any means now at length I might have a prosperous journey by the will of God to come unto you." The brief verse notes Paul's prayer to visit Rome. The grammatical voices note the longing. Paul prayed that, by some means and at last, he might have a prosperous journey to come to the Romans — a long-cherished desire.

On "by the will of God," the witnesses note the submission. Calvin and others note Paul subordinated his desire to God's will — longing to come, yet submitting the matter to God's good pleasure. He would come if and as God willed.

The witnesses note the providence: Paul would indeed come to Rome, though by a way he did not expect — as a prisoner (Acts 28). The verse displays Paul's submissive prayer to visit Rome.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Paul prayed that, by some means and at last, he might have a prosperous journey, by the will of God, to come to the Romans — a long-cherished desire, submitted to God's good pleasure (Calvin: he would come if and as God willed). The verse displays Paul's submissive prayer to visit Rome. The verse marks the longing: at last to come to them. The verse displays the submission: by the will of God. The verse displays the providence: Paul would come, though as a prisoner (Acts 28). The verse displays the apostle's desire: long-cherished, yet submitted. Paul's submissive prayer to visit Rome displays both his longing and his submission to God's will. The verse displays the balance: earnest desire, yet "if God will." The verse displays the providence: God's way differing from Paul's expectation. The verse displays the model of prayer: longing submitted to God's pleasure. Paul's submissive prayer to come to Rome displays his earnest desire to see the church, joined with humble submission to the will of God — a model of prayer that asks earnestly yet yields wholly to God's good pleasure. The verse displays the providence that would bring Paul to Rome, though by an unexpected path. The verse sets up his account of why he longed to come (vv.11-15).
11:
"For I long to see you, that I may impart unto you some spiritual gift, to the end ye may be established;" The witnesses note Paul's longing and its purpose. On "I long to see you," the grammatical voices note the affection. Paul yearned to see the Romans — a deep, tender longing for a church he had not met.

On "some spiritual gift," the witnesses note the purpose. Barnes and Calvin note Paul desired to impart some spiritual gift (charisma) — some grace, instruction, or benefit for their edification, not material but spiritual.

On "to the end ye may be established," the witnesses note the goal. The aim was their establishment — confirming and strengthening them in the faith. Calvin notes Paul's modesty: in v.12 he softens this, lest he seem to assume superiority. The verse displays Paul's longing to impart a spiritual gift for their establishment.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul longed to see the Romans (a deep, tender affection) that he might impart some spiritual gift (charisma, some grace or benefit, not material but spiritual, Barnes, Calvin) to the end they might be established — confirmed and strengthened in the faith. The verse displays Paul's longing to impart a spiritual gift for their establishment. The verse marks the affection: yearning to see them. The verse displays the purpose: imparting a spiritual gift. The verse displays the goal: their establishment in the faith. The verse displays the spiritual aim: edification, not material benefit. Paul's longing to impart a spiritual gift displays both his love and his pastoral purpose. The verse displays the apostle's heart: yearning for their strengthening. The verse displays the nature of the gift: spiritual, for edification. The verse displays the goal: establishing them firmly. Paul's longing to see the Romans and impart a spiritual gift displays his deep affection and his pastoral aim: to strengthen and establish them in the faith. The verse displays the apostle's desire to benefit the church spiritually, confirming them in the gospel. The verse sets up his modest qualification: the benefit would be mutual (v.12).
12:
"That is, that I may be comforted together with you by the mutual faith both of you and me." The witnesses note Paul's modest correction: the benefit would be mutual. On the softening, the grammatical voices note the humility. Calvin, Barnes, and Robertson note Paul, having spoken of imparting a gift (v.11), gracefully corrects himself, lest he seem to assume superiority — the comfort would be mutual, drawn from the faith they shared.

On "mutual faith," the witnesses note the fellowship. Paul and the Romans would be mutually comforted by each other's faith — the apostle himself encouraged by their faith, as they by his. Lightfoot and others note the tender humility.

The witnesses note the model: even the great apostle sought comfort from the faith of ordinary believers — a lesson in mutual edification and humility. The verse displays Paul's modest correction: mutual comfort by shared faith.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul gracefully corrects himself (lest he seem to assume superiority, Calvin, Barnes, Robertson) — the comfort would be mutual, he and the Romans encouraged together by their shared faith, the apostle himself drawing comfort from their faith as they from his. The verse displays Paul's modest correction: mutual comfort by shared faith. The verse marks the humility: softening the claim of v.11. The verse displays the mutual benefit: comfort flowing both ways. The verse displays the fellowship: shared faith encouraging both. The verse displays the model: even the apostle comforted by ordinary believers' faith. Paul's modest correction displays his humility and the principle of mutual edification. The verse displays the tender fellowship: apostle and church encouraging one another. The verse displays the lesson: the strong comforted by the faith of all. The verse displays the grace of Paul: not assuming superiority but seeking mutual benefit. Paul's modest correction — that the comfort would be mutual, by their shared faith — displays his humility and the beautiful principle of mutual edification in the body of Christ, where even the great apostle is encouraged by the faith of ordinary believers. The verse displays the fellowship of faith, each member strengthening the others. The verse sets up Paul's account of his repeated purpose to visit them (v.13).
13:
"Now I would not have you ignorant, brethren, that oftentimes I purposed to come unto you, (but was let hitherto,) that I might have some fruit among you also, even as among other Gentiles." The witnesses note Paul's repeated, hindered purpose to visit Rome. On "oftentimes I purposed to come," the grammatical voices note the persistence. Paul had often planned to visit the Romans, his desire long-standing.

On "but was let hitherto," the witnesses note the hindrance. Robertson and others note "let" means hindered (the old English sense) — Paul was prevented until now, by various providences (his work elsewhere, Acts 16:6-7).

On "that I might have some fruit," the witnesses note the purpose. Paul desired fruit among the Romans — converts, edification, the harvest of his ministry — as among the other Gentiles. The verse displays Paul's repeated, hindered purpose to bear fruit at Rome.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: Paul had often purposed to come to the Romans but was hindered ("let," in the old sense, prevented until now by various providences, his work elsewhere, Robertson) — desiring to have some fruit (converts, edification, the harvest of his ministry) among them as among the other Gentiles. The verse displays Paul's repeated, hindered purpose to bear fruit at Rome. The verse marks the persistence: oftentimes purposing. The verse displays the hindrance: prevented until now. The verse displays the purpose: fruit among them. The verse displays the providence: the hindrances of God's appointing. Paul's repeated, hindered purpose displays both his earnest desire and his submission to providence. The verse displays the apostle's aim: fruit, the harvest of ministry. The verse displays the providential delays: prevented for God's reasons. The verse displays the parallel: fruit at Rome as among other Gentiles. Paul's repeated, hindered purpose to visit Rome displays his earnest, long-standing desire to bear fruit among the Romans and his submission to the providences that delayed him. The verse displays the apostle's missionary heart: longing for the harvest at Rome as among all the Gentiles, yet yielding to God's timing. The verse sets up his declaration of obligation to all (v.14) and his readiness to preach (v.15).
14:
"I am debtor both to the Greeks, and to the Barbarians; both to the wise, and to the unwise." The witnesses note Paul's universal obligation. On "debtor," the grammatical voices note the force. Barnes and Robertson note Paul regarded himself as under obligation (a debtor) to preach to all — having received the gospel and his commission, he owed its proclamation to all classes.

On "Greeks and Barbarians, wise and unwise," the witnesses note the comprehensive scope. The two pairs encompass all mankind: Greeks (the cultured) and Barbarians (the non-Greek), wise and unwise — none excluded from Paul's obligation.

The witnesses note the apostle's sense of stewardship: the gospel a debt he owed to all, regardless of culture or learning. The verse displays Paul's universal obligation to preach to all.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Paul regarded himself a debtor (under obligation, having received the gospel and his commission, Barnes, Robertson) to all — Greeks and Barbarians, wise and unwise — the two pairs encompassing all mankind, none excluded. The verse displays Paul's universal obligation to preach to all. The verse marks the sense of debt: the gospel an obligation owed. The verse displays the comprehensive scope: all classes and cultures. The verse displays the stewardship: Paul owing the gospel to all. The verse displays the inclusiveness: wise and unwise alike. Paul's universal obligation displays his sense of stewardship and the gospel's reach to all. The verse displays the debtor's heart: owing the gospel to every class. The verse displays the breadth: Greek and Barbarian, learned and simple. The verse displays the apostle's commission: to all mankind. Paul's universal obligation to preach to Greeks and Barbarians, wise and unwise, displays his profound sense of stewardship — the gospel a debt he owed to all people, regardless of culture or learning. The verse displays the inclusiveness of the gospel and the apostle's readiness to serve all, the foundation of his eagerness to preach even at Rome (v.15). The verse displays the debt of love that drove Paul's mission to the whole world. The debt of love that drove the apostle to the whole world is here laid open in a single, sweeping confession of obligation to all mankind.
15:
"So, as much as in me is, I am ready to preach the gospel to you that are at Rome also." The brief verse notes Paul's readiness to preach at Rome. The grammatical voices note the eagerness. Paul, as far as in him lay, was ready and eager to preach the gospel even at Rome — the proud capital of the empire.

On "ready to preach," the witnesses note the resolve. Despite the grandeur and hostility of Rome, Paul was eager to proclaim the gospel there, undeterred by its splendor or its scorn.

The Reformed Calvin and others note the boldness: Paul ready to bring the despised gospel to the seat of worldly power, not ashamed (v.16). The verse displays Paul's readiness to preach at Rome.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: Paul, as far as in him lay, was ready and eager to preach the gospel even at Rome — the proud capital of the empire, undeterred by its splendor or scorn (Calvin: ready to bring the despised gospel to the seat of worldly power). The verse displays Paul's readiness to preach at Rome. The verse marks the eagerness: ready to preach. The verse displays the boldness: undaunted by Rome's grandeur. The verse displays the resolve: eager even at the capital. The verse displays the courage: bringing the gospel to the seat of power. Paul's readiness to preach at Rome displays his eagerness and boldness. The verse displays the apostle's zeal: undeterred by Rome's splendor or hostility. The verse displays the resolve to preach the despised gospel at the world's center. The verse sets up the great theme: he is not ashamed of the gospel (v.16). Paul's readiness to preach the gospel at Rome displays his eager zeal and undaunted courage, ready to proclaim the despised gospel even in the proud capital of the empire. The verse displays the apostle's boldness, leading directly into his great declaration that he is not ashamed of the gospel, for it is the power of God unto salvation (v.16). The verse marks the transition from the personal preface to the doctrinal theme of the epistle.
16:
"For I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ: for it is the power of God unto salvation to every one that believeth; to the Jew first, and also to the Greek." The witnesses note the great theme verse. On "not ashamed," the grammatical voices note the boldness. Barnes and Henry note Paul, though the gospel was a stumbling-block to Jews and folly to Greeks (the cross, a crucified Savior), gloried in it — not ashamed even at Rome.

On "the power of God unto salvation," the witnesses note the gospel's efficacy. Robertson and Calvin note the gospel is God's power (dynamis) — the mighty agency by which God saves; not a dead letter but the means by which divine power goes forth to save all who believe.

On "to every one that believeth; to the Jew first," the witnesses note the condition and order. Faith is the condition; and the gospel came first to the Jew (in privilege and order, Acts 13:46), then to the Greek (all the Gentiles) — the priority of the Jew, yet the universal reach. The verse displays the gospel as the saving power of God to all who believe.

The witnesses agree: Paul is not ashamed of the gospel (though a stumbling-block to Jews and folly to Greeks, the cross, he glories in it, Barnes, Henry), for it is the power of God (dynamis, the mighty saving agency, not a dead letter, Robertson, Calvin) unto salvation to every one that believes — faith the condition — to the Jew first (in privilege and order, Acts 13:46) and also to the Greek (all the Gentiles). The verse displays the gospel as the saving power of God to all who believe. The verse marks the theme: the gospel the power of God. The verse displays the boldness: not ashamed. The verse displays the condition: to every one that believes. The verse displays the order: to the Jew first, then the Greek. The gospel as the power of God unto salvation displays its divine efficacy and universal reach. The verse displays faith as the condition of salvation. The verse displays the priority of the Jew, yet the universal scope. The verse displays the apostle's confidence: glorying in the gospel. The gospel as the power of God unto salvation to every one that believes, to the Jew first and also to the Greek, displays the theme of the whole epistle: the divine, saving power of the gospel, received by faith, offered to all — the Jew first in privilege, yet the Gentiles equally welcome. The verse displays Paul's bold confidence in the gospel and its universal, saving efficacy. The verse sets up the explanation: the righteousness of God revealed (v.17).
17:
"For therein is the righteousness of God revealed from faith to faith: as it is written, The just shall live by faith." The witnesses note the central crux of the epistle: the righteousness of God. On "the righteousness of God," the grammatical and Reformed voices debate. Barnes, Calvin, and Ellicott take it as God's plan or method of justifying men by faith (not his attribute of justice, nor mere goodness) — God's way of treating men as righteous, the way of justification revealed in the gospel. The Reformed Gill and Henry stress imputed righteousness: the righteousness Christ wrought by obedience and death, imputed to believers, called "of God" because of its author and approval.

On "from faith to faith," the witnesses divide. Some (Calvin: by faith, revealed unto faith; Robertson: faith the start and goal; Lightfoot) read it as faith first to last; others (Henry, Clarke) as from Old-Testament faith to New, or increasing faith. All agree faith is all in all.

On "the just shall live by faith," the witnesses note the proof from Habakkuk 2:4. Calvin and Robertson note Paul grounds justification by faith on the prophet: the righteous live by faith — not by works, but by trust in God. The verse displays the righteousness of God revealed, received by faith.

The witnesses agree the heart of the gospel is here: the righteousness of God revealed from faith to faith, proved by Habakkuk's "the just shall live by faith." The grammatical-historical voices (Barnes, Ellicott, Calvin) take "righteousness of God" as God's plan/method of justifying men by faith — God's way of accounting men righteous, not his attribute of justice; the Reformed Gill and Henry stress the imputed righteousness of Christ, called "of God" by its author and approval; Wesley names both the essential righteousness and the righteousness of faith. "From faith to faith" is read as faith first to last (Calvin, Robertson) or Old- to New-Testament faith (Henry, Clarke). The verse displays the righteousness of God revealed, received by faith. The verse marks the central doctrine: justification by faith. The verse displays the crux: what the "righteousness of God" means. The verse displays the proof: Habakkuk's "the just shall live by faith." The verse displays the universality of faith: all in all. The righteousness of God revealed from faith to faith displays the heart of the gospel: God's way of justifying sinners by faith, grounded in the prophets. The verse displays the great Reformation text. The verse displays the means: faith, from first to last. The verse displays the foundation: the just shall live by faith. The righteousness of God revealed from faith to faith, proved by "the just shall live by faith," displays the cardinal doctrine of the epistle and the gospel: that God justifies sinners not by works but by faith, accounting them righteous through Christ. The verse displays the heart of Paul's gospel, the great text of the Reformation, the foundation of salvation by grace through faith.
18:
"For the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of men, who hold the truth in unrighteousness;" The witnesses note the revelation of God's wrath — the dark counterpart to the righteousness of v.17. On "the wrath of God," the grammatical voices note the meaning. Barnes, Clarke, and Robertson note "wrath" (orge), applied to God, is not human passion or revenge but his settled, holy displeasure against sin — the determination of the divine mind to punish iniquity (Robertson: "the wrath of reason and law," not rage).

On "ungodliness and unrighteousness," the witnesses note the two heads. Henry, Ellicott, and others note ungodliness (against God, the first table) and unrighteousness (against men, the second table) — sin against God and neighbor.

On "hold the truth in unrighteousness," the witnesses note the suppression. Robertson, Ellicott, and Clarke note "hold" means hold down, suppress, hinder — wicked men suppress the truth they have (conscience, natural revelation) by their unrighteousness, imprisoning it lest it restrain them. The verse displays the wrath of God revealed against those who suppress the truth.

The witnesses agree: the wrath of God (orge — not passion or revenge but his settled, holy displeasure against sin, "the wrath of reason and law," Barnes, Clarke, Robertson) is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness (against God) and unrighteousness (against men, the two tables, Henry, Ellicott) of those who hold (hold down, suppress, hinder) the truth in unrighteousness — wicked men imprisoning the truth they have lest it restrain them (Robertson, Ellicott, Clarke). The verse displays the wrath of God revealed against those who suppress the truth. The verse marks the dark counterpart to v.17: wrath beside righteousness. The verse displays the nature of God's wrath: holy displeasure, not passion. The verse displays the two heads of sin: ungodliness and unrighteousness. The verse displays the suppression: holding down the truth. The wrath of God revealed against suppressed truth displays both the holiness of God and the guilt of men. The verse displays the proof of wrath: in the degradation of sin (v.24ff). The verse displays the culpability: suppressing known truth. The verse displays the antithesis: righteousness revealed unto life, wrath revealed against sin. The wrath of God revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness, against those who suppress the truth, displays the holy displeasure of God against sin and the guilt of men who imprison the truth they have. The verse opens the great argument that all are under sin and need the righteousness of God, beginning with the Gentile world (vv.19-32).
19:
"Because that which may be known of God is manifest in them; for God hath shewed it unto them." The witnesses note the manifestation of God to the heathen. On "that which may be known of God," the grammatical voices note the natural knowledge. Barnes, Robertson, and Calvin note there is a knowledge of God (his existence and many perfections) available to all, manifest within and around them — not the full saving knowledge, but enough to leave them without excuse.

On "manifest in them; for God hath shewed it," the witnesses note the divine source. The puritan Henry and Calvin note God himself has shown this knowledge to all — implanting an awareness of his being, and displaying his glory in creation. The knowledge is God's own revelation in nature and conscience.

The Reformed Calvin notes the careful limit: God has presented to all the means of knowing him, so manifesting himself by his works that men must see there is a God; but this knowledge avails only to remove excuse, not to save. The verse displays the manifestation of God to the heathen.

The witnesses agree: that which may be known of God (his existence and many perfections, the natural knowledge available to all, not the full saving knowledge, Barnes, Robertson, Calvin) is manifest in them, for God himself has shown it — implanting an awareness of his being and displaying his glory in creation (Henry, Calvin). Calvin notes the careful limit: God so manifests himself by his works that men must see there is a God, but this knowledge avails only to remove excuse, not to save. The verse displays the manifestation of God to the heathen. The verse marks the natural knowledge: available to all. The verse displays the divine source: God has shown it. The verse displays the limit: enough to condemn, not to save. The verse displays the means: nature and conscience. The manifestation of God to the heathen displays both the reality of natural revelation and its limit. The verse displays God's witness in all: the implanted sense of deity. The verse displays the foundation of the heathen's guilt: they have the knowledge. The verse displays the careful distinction: natural knowledge condemns but does not save. The manifestation of God to all men, shown by God himself in nature and conscience, displays the reality of natural revelation — enough to leave the heathen without excuse, though not enough to save. The verse displays the ground of the Gentiles' guilt: they had the knowledge of God and suppressed it. The verse sets up the fuller statement of natural revelation (v.20).
20:
"For the invisible things of him from the creation of the world are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made, even his eternal power and Godhead; so that they are without excuse:" The witnesses note the proof from creation, leaving men without excuse. On "the invisible things... clearly seen by the things that are made," the grammatical voices note the play on words. Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson note God's invisible attributes are discerned by the mind through the visible creation — the unseen seen through the seen, the phenomena of nature.

On "his eternal power and Godhead," the witnesses note the attributes revealed. Robertson and Ellicott distinguish: theiotes (divinity, divine nature/properties) rather than theotes (deity, personality) — the eternal power and divine nature displayed in creation, knowable apart from special revelation.

On "so that they are without excuse," the witnesses note the conclusion. The Reformed Calvin notes the design: the manifestation is clear enough that men cannot plead ignorance; though by our blindness it suffices not to save, we are not so blind as to have any excuse for our perverseness. The verse displays the proof from creation, leaving men inexcusable.

The witnesses agree: God's invisible attributes — his eternal power and Godhead (theiotes, divine nature/properties, not theotes, deity/personality, Robertson, Ellicott) — are clearly seen, discerned by the mind through the visible creation (the unseen seen through the seen, Barnes, Ellicott), so that men are without excuse. Calvin notes the design: the manifestation is clear enough that men cannot plead ignorance; though by our blindness it suffices not to save, we are not so blind as to excuse our perverseness. The verse displays the proof from creation, leaving men inexcusable. The verse marks the natural revelation: God known through his works. The verse displays the attributes: eternal power and divine nature. The verse displays the conclusion: without excuse. The verse displays the play: the invisible seen through the visible. The proof from creation, leaving men inexcusable, displays both the reality of natural revelation and the guilt of the heathen. The verse displays the witness of creation: the works declaring the Maker. The verse displays the careful limit (Calvin): enough to remove excuse, not to save. The verse displays the verdict: inexcusable. The proof from creation — God's eternal power and Godhead clearly seen through the things made — displays the reality of natural revelation and leaves all men without excuse for their idolatry and impiety. The verse displays the foundation of the Gentiles' condemnation: they had clear evidence of God in creation, yet turned from him. The verse sets up the account of their declension into idolatry (vv.21-23).
21:
"Because that, when they knew God, they glorified him not as God, neither were thankful; but became vain in their imaginations, and their foolish heart was darkened." The witnesses note the heathen's declension from knowledge into folly. On "when they knew God," the grammatical voices note the original knowledge. Barnes and Robertson note the heathen had a knowledge of God (the philosophers especially knew one God), the reason for their condemnation — they failed to act on what they knew.

On "glorified him not... neither were thankful," the witnesses note the root sin. They did not honor God as God nor give thanks — ingratitude and refusal to glorify him the source of their corruption (Barnes: an unwillingness to honor God hardens the heart).

On "became vain... foolish heart darkened," the witnesses note the consequence. Robertson and Calvin note their reasonings (the vain speculations of the philosophers) became empty, and their senseless heart (kardia, the whole inner man) was darkened — folly and darkness following the refusal to glorify God. The verse displays the heathen's declension from knowledge into darkness.

The witnesses agree: the heathen, when they knew God (had a real knowledge of him, the philosophers especially, Barnes, Robertson), glorified him not as God nor gave thanks — ingratitude and refusal to honor him the root of their corruption — but became vain in their reasonings (the empty speculations of the philosophers, Robertson, Calvin) and their foolish heart (the whole inner man) was darkened. The verse displays the heathen's declension from knowledge into darkness. The verse marks the original knowledge: they knew God. The verse displays the root sin: not glorifying or thanking him. The verse displays the consequence: vain reasonings, darkened heart. The verse displays the process: from knowledge to folly. The heathen's declension displays the path from refused knowledge to spiritual darkness. The verse displays the culpability: knowing yet not honoring God. The verse displays the result: folly and darkness. The verse displays the root: ingratitude and refusal to glorify God. The heathen's declension from knowledge into darkness displays the path of apostasy: knowing God yet refusing to glorify or thank him, they fell into vain reasonings and a darkened heart. The verse displays the source of idolatry and corruption: not ignorance but the willful refusal to honor the God they knew. The verse sets up their descent into idolatry (vv.22-23).
22:
"Professing themselves to be wise, they became fools," The brief verse notes the heathen's self-conceited folly. On "professing to be wise," the grammatical voices note the boast. Barnes and Robertson note the philosophers boasted of wisdom (the very word "philosopher" means lover of wisdom) — yet their speculations and idolatry proved them fools.

On "they became fools," the witnesses note the judicial folly. Ellicott and Robertson note the folly was itself judicially inflicted — they were "made fools," their pretended wisdom turning to folly (an oxymoron, the wise made foolish, Robertson).

The Reformed Calvin notes the universality: not only the philosophers, but all men, sought to be wise of themselves, drawing God down to their own conception — the arrogance condemned here is innate, the wiser men thought themselves, the more foolish they became. The verse displays the heathen's self-conceited folly.

The very brief comment matches the brief verse. The witnesses agree: the heathen, professing to be wise (the philosophers boasting of wisdom, Barnes, Robertson), became fools — the folly judicially inflicted, they were "made fools," their pretended wisdom turning to folly (an oxymoron, Ellicott, Robertson). Calvin notes the universality: not only philosophers but all men sought to be wise of themselves, drawing God down to their conception — the wiser they thought themselves, the more foolish they became. The verse displays the heathen's self-conceited folly. The verse marks the boast: professing wisdom. The verse displays the result: became fools. The verse displays the judicial folly: made fools. The verse displays the irony: the wise made foolish. The heathen's self-conceited folly displays the emptiness of wisdom apart from God. The verse displays the universality (Calvin): all men prone to this conceit. The verse displays the judgment: folly inflicted on pride. The verse displays the oxymoron: the wise become fools. The heathen's self-conceited folly — professing wisdom, they became fools — displays the emptiness of human wisdom that turns from God, and the judicial folly inflicted on those who, in their pride, sought to be wise apart from their Maker. The verse displays the truth that the higher the nations' refinement, the more degraded their idolatry (Hodge), and sets up the description of that idolatry (v.23).
23:
"And changed the glory of the uncorruptible God into an image made like to corruptible man, and to birds, and fourfooted beasts, and creeping things." The witnesses note the descent into idolatry. On "changed the glory," the grammatical voices note the exchange. Barnes and Calvin note they exchanged the glory of the incorruptible God for images — not changing God himself, but forsaking him for idols, a disgraceful bargain (the truth of God for a lie).

On "an image like to corruptible man... birds... beasts... creeping things," the witnesses note the degradation. Barnes, Clarke, and Ellicott note the descending scale of idolatry: from images of man (the Greek and Roman gods in human form) down to birds (the Egyptian ibis, hawk), beasts (the ox, apis), and creeping things (the crocodile, serpent) — the deeper the degradation, the lower the object.

The eastern Theodoret notes the folly: they worshipped as gods the very creatures men eat, loathe, or kill — the height of madness. The verse displays the descent into idolatry, exchanging God's glory for images.

The witnesses agree: the heathen exchanged the glory of the incorruptible God for images (not changing God himself but forsaking him, the truth of God for a lie, a disgraceful bargain, Barnes, Calvin) — a descending scale from images of corruptible man (the Greek and Roman gods in human form) down to birds (the Egyptian ibis, hawk), four-footed beasts (the ox, apis), and creeping things (the crocodile, serpent, Barnes, Clarke, Ellicott). Theodoret notes the folly: they worshipped as gods the very creatures men eat, loathe, or kill — the height of madness. The verse displays the descent into idolatry. The verse marks the exchange: God's glory for images. The verse displays the degradation: the descending scale of idols. The verse displays the folly: worshipping creatures. The verse displays the disgrace: the incorruptible for the corruptible. The descent into idolatry displays the depth of the heathen's degradation. The verse displays the exchange: forsaking God for images. The verse displays the scale: man, birds, beasts, creeping things. The verse displays the madness: deifying base creatures. The descent into idolatry — exchanging the glory of the incorruptible God for images of corruptible man, birds, beasts, and creeping things — displays the depth of the heathen's degradation, forsaking the living God for lifeless and base idols. The verse displays the consequence of refusing to glorify God: the descent into ever lower idolatry, the exchange of the truth of God for a lie. The verse sets up the first "God gave them up" (v.24).
24:
"Wherefore God also gave them up to uncleanness through the lusts of their own hearts, to dishonour their own bodies between themselves:" The witnesses note the first "God gave them up" — and the meaning of the giving up. On "God gave them up" (paredoken), the grammatical voices note the abandonment. Barnes, Robertson, and Theodoret note God abandoned them, withdrew his restraint, and suffered them to follow their own lusts — not actively inducing sin, but leaving them to themselves (Theodoret: gave them up = permitted; Robertson: the withdrawal of God's restraint sent men deeper down).

On the depth of the judgment, the Reformed Calvin presses further: God not only permits but, by his just judgment, so arranges that men are carried into sin by their own lusts and by Satan (his executioner) — yet God is not the author of sin, whose roots abide in the sinner. The verse marks the debate: permissive withdrawal vs efficient judgment.

On "uncleanness... dishonour their own bodies," the witnesses note the result. The abandonment issued in uncleanness — the degrading of the body through their own lusts. The verse displays the first giving up: to uncleanness.

The witnesses agree the first "God gave them up" (paredoken) marks a judicial abandonment — but divide on its nature. The grammatical-historical voices (Barnes, Robertson) and Theodoret take it as permission/withdrawal of restraint: God left them to their own lusts, not actively inducing sin (Theodoret: gave up = permitted; Robertson: withdrawal of restraint sent men deeper). The Reformed Calvin presses further: more than bare permission — God by his just judgment so arranges that men are carried into sin by their lusts and by Satan his executioner, yet God is not the author of sin, whose roots abide in the sinner. The result: uncleanness, the dishonoring of their own bodies. The verse displays the first giving up. The verse marks the judicial abandonment. The verse displays the debate: permission vs efficient judgment. The verse displays the result: uncleanness. The verse displays the principle: sin punished by sin. The first giving up displays God's judicial abandonment of the heathen to their own lusts. The verse displays the withdrawal of restraint. The verse displays the careful distinction: God not the author of sin. The verse displays the consequence of idolatry: moral corruption. The first "God gave them up" to uncleanness displays the judicial abandonment of the heathen — whether by permissive withdrawal (Barnes, Theodoret) or efficient judgment (Calvin) — leaving them to the lusts of their own hearts and the dishonoring of their bodies, the moral consequence of their idolatry. The verse displays the solemn principle that sin is punished by sin, God withdrawing his restraint from those who forsake him.
25:
"Who changed the truth of God into a lie, and worshipped and served the creature more than the Creator, who is blessed for ever. Amen." The witnesses note the heart of idolatry and Paul's doxology. On "changed the truth of God into a lie," the grammatical voices note the exchange. Barnes, Robertson, and Calvin note "the truth of God" means the true God, exchanged for "a lie" (false gods, idols) — a Hebraism, the true God for the false.

On "worshipped and served the creature more than the Creator," the witnesses note the inversion. Robertson and Calvin note "more than" (para) means rather than, to the neglect of — they gave to the creature the worship due the Creator alone, the fundamental inversion of idolatry.

On "who is blessed for ever. Amen," the witnesses note the doxology. Ellicott and Robertson note Paul, shocked at the language of idolatry he must use, turns aside in reverent adoration — blessing the Creator whose honor the idolaters robbed. Theodoret notes the warning against those who worship a creature as God. The verse displays the inversion of idolatry and Paul's reverent doxology.

The witnesses agree: the heathen changed the truth of God into a lie (the true God exchanged for false gods, a Hebraism, Barnes, Robertson, Calvin) and worshipped and served the creature rather than (para, to the neglect of) the Creator — the fundamental inversion of idolatry, giving the creature the worship due God alone. Paul, shocked at the language he must use, turns aside in reverent doxology: "who is blessed for ever. Amen" (Ellicott, Robertson), blessing the Creator the idolaters robbed. Theodoret warns against worshipping any creature as God. The verse displays the inversion of idolatry and Paul's doxology. The verse marks the exchange: the true God for a lie. The verse displays the inversion: the creature served rather than the Creator. The verse displays the doxology: reverent adoration. The verse displays the contrast: the blessed Creator vs the worshipped creature. The inversion of idolatry, and Paul's doxology, display both the depth of the heathen's error and the apostle's reverence. The verse displays the heart of idolatry: serving the creature. The verse displays the reverence of Paul: blessing God amid describing blasphemy. The verse displays the warning: against deifying the creature. The inversion of idolatry — worshipping the creature rather than the Creator, exchanging the truth of God for a lie — displays the fundamental error of the heathen, and Paul's reverent doxology displays his adoration of the Creator whom the idolaters dishonored. The verse displays the apostle's instinctive turn to praise, blessing forever the God whose glory idolatry robbed. The verse sets up the second "God gave them up" (v.26).
26:
"For this cause God gave them up unto vile affections: for even their women did change the natural use into that which is against nature:" The witnesses note the second "God gave them up" — to vile affections. On "for this cause," the grammatical voices note the connection. Barnes and Robertson note this is the consequence of their idolatry: because they exchanged God for idols, God gave them up to degrading passions (the practical fruit of their false belief).

On "vile affections... against nature," the witnesses note the degradation. Robertson, Calvin, and Theodoret note the passions of dishonor, contrary to nature — Paul, in describing the state of the heathen world, must charge these shameful sins, abundantly attested by the heathen writers themselves.

The eastern Chrysostom notes the gravity: these affections were vile, the soul more dishonored than the body in disease; and Paul deprives them of excuse by noting they "changed" the natural use, having known it. The verse displays the second giving up: to vile affections against nature.

The witnesses agree: for the cause of their idolatry (because they exchanged God for idols, Barnes, Robertson), God gave them up a second time — to vile affections, passions of dishonor contrary to nature (Robertson, Calvin, Theodoret), the practical fruit of their false belief, sins abundantly attested by the heathen writers themselves. Chrysostom notes the gravity: these affections were vile, the soul more dishonored than the body in disease; Paul deprives them of excuse, noting they "changed" the natural use, having known it. The verse displays the second giving up: to vile affections against nature. The verse marks the connection: idolatry issuing in degradation. The verse displays the judgment: given up to vile affections. The verse displays the gravity: against nature. The verse displays the culpability: changing the natural use. The second giving up displays the moral consequence of idolatry: degrading passions. The verse displays the principle: false worship issuing in moral ruin. The verse displays the depth: contrary to nature. The verse displays the apostle's faithful diagnosis of the heathen world. The second "God gave them up" to vile affections displays the moral consequence of idolatry — the degradation of the natural order into that which is against nature, the practical fruit of exchanging the Creator for the creature. The verse displays Paul's faithful, sober diagnosis of the depth of the heathen world's corruption, and the judicial abandonment that left them to dishonor. The verse continues the description (v.27).
27:
"And likewise also the men, leaving the natural use of the woman, burned in their lust one toward another; men with men working that which is unseemly, and receiving in themselves that recompence of their error which was meet." The witnesses note the continuation of the degradation and its recompense. On the description, the grammatical voices note Paul's faithful charge. Barnes and Robertson note Paul must, for his argument, charge the heathen world with these shameful sins — degrading and unnatural, yet abundantly documented in the Greek and Roman writers themselves (Barnes cites extensive evidence).

On "receiving in themselves that recompence," the witnesses note the just penalty. Ellicott, Clarke, and Theodoret note the recompense of their error (their turning from God to idols) was seen in these unnatural excesses — sin its own punishment, the very degradation a judgment, "the recompense which was meet."

The eastern Theodoret notes: infamy is the severest penalty of such affections; what no victorious enemy could inflict, they willingly ran into, bringing on themselves a punishment heavier than any judge would impose. The verse displays the continued degradation and its self-inflicted recompense.

The witnesses agree: Paul, for his argument, faithfully charges the heathen world with these shameful, unnatural sins — degrading, yet abundantly documented in the Greek and Roman writers themselves (Barnes, Robertson) — and notes they received in themselves "that recompence of their error which was meet": the recompense of their turning from God to idols seen in these excesses, sin its own punishment (Ellicott, Clarke, Theodoret). Theodoret notes infamy is the severest penalty; what no enemy could inflict, they willingly ran into, a punishment heavier than any judge would impose. The verse displays the continued degradation and its self-inflicted recompense. The verse marks Paul's faithful charge: the heathen's shameful sins. The verse displays the recompense: sin its own penalty. The verse displays the justice: the recompense which was meet. The verse displays the principle: the error punished in the degradation. The continued degradation and its recompense display the just judgment of God: sin punished by sin. The verse displays the documentation: attested by the heathen writers. The verse displays the self-infliction: bringing the penalty on themselves. The verse displays the connection: the error (idolatry) and its recompense (degradation). The continued degradation of the heathen and its self-inflicted recompense display the just judgment of God, whereby their turning from the Creator was punished in the very excesses to which they were abandoned — sin becoming its own penalty, the recompense which was meet. The verse displays Paul's faithful, sober diagnosis and the divine justice that lets sin reap its own degradation. The verse sets up the third "God gave them up" (v.28).
28:
"And even as they did not like to retain God in their knowledge, God gave them over to a reprobate mind, to do those things which are not convenient;" The witnesses note the third "God gave them up" — to a reprobate mind. On the play on words, the grammatical voices note the correspondence. Robertson, Ellicott, and Calvin note the wordplay: as they did not approve (edokimasan) to retain God, God gave them to a reprobate (adokimon) mind — they rejected God, and God rejected their mind, giving them a mind disapproved, void of judgment.

On "did not like to retain God," the witnesses note the willful rejection. Barnes and Clarke note it was not that they could not, but that they were displeased with God and chose to forsake him — a willful, deliberate rejection (Clarke: they did not search to retain God).

On "a reprobate mind... things not convenient," the witnesses note the result. The eastern Chrysostom notes the sin belonged to the mind, not the flesh — the fount of the evils flowed from a corrupted, undiscerning mind, leading to deeds not fitting. The verse displays the third giving up: to a reprobate mind.

The witnesses agree: the third "God gave them up" turns on a wordplay — as they did not approve (edokimasan) to retain God in their knowledge, God gave them over to a reprobate (adokimon) mind, a mind disapproved and void of judgment (Robertson, Ellicott, Calvin); a willful rejection, not inability but displeasure with God (Barnes, Clarke), issuing in deeds not fitting. Chrysostom notes the sin belonged to the mind, not the flesh — the fount of evils flowing from a corrupted, undiscerning mind. The verse displays the third giving up: to a reprobate mind. The verse marks the wordplay: rejecting God, rejected to a reprobate mind. The verse displays the willful rejection: not liking to retain God. The verse displays the result: a mind void of judgment. The verse displays the deeds: things not convenient. The third giving up displays the just correspondence: sin met with judgment in kind. The verse displays the willfulness: deliberate rejection of God. The verse displays the consequence: a reprobate, undiscerning mind. The verse displays the source: the corrupted mind the fount of evils. The third "God gave them up" to a reprobate mind displays the just correspondence between sin and judgment: as men rejected the knowledge of God, God gave them to a mind incapable of discerning right, the fount from which the catalogue of vices (vv.29-31) would flow. The verse displays the climax of the judicial abandonment: a depraved mind, void of judgment, abandoned to do what is not fitting. The verse sets up the catalogue of sins (vv.29-31).
29:
"Being filled with all unrighteousness, fornication, wickedness, covetousness, maliciousness; full of envy, murder, debate, deceit, malignity; whisperers," The witnesses note the catalogue of sins flowing from the reprobate mind. On "being filled with all unrighteousness," the grammatical voices note the comprehensiveness. Chrysostom and Robertson note the intensive force: "filled," "all" — the reprobate mind issuing in every kind of wickedness, the heathen world saturated with sin.

On the list, the witnesses note the catalogue. Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson note the descending list of vices — unrighteousness, wickedness, covetousness, maliciousness, envy, murder, debate (strife), deceit, malignity, whisperers (secret slanderers) — the practical fruit of the abandoned mind. (The best manuscripts omit "fornication," Ellicott.)

The Reformed Calvin notes Paul set down these vices as they occurred, not in strict order; none was free from all, though not all guilty of each — every one chargeable with some. The verse displays the catalogue of sins from the reprobate mind.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the reprobate mind issued in being "filled with all unrighteousness" — the intensive force, the heathen world saturated with sin (Chrysostom, Robertson) — followed by a catalogue: wickedness, covetousness, maliciousness, envy, murder, debate (strife), deceit, malignity, whisperers (secret slanderers), the practical fruit of the abandoned mind (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson; the best manuscripts omitting "fornication"). Calvin notes Paul set these down as they occurred, not in strict order; none free from all, though not all guilty of each. The verse displays the catalogue of sins. The verse marks the comprehensiveness: filled with all unrighteousness. The verse displays the list: the vices of the abandoned mind. The verse displays the saturation: the heathen world full of sin. The verse displays the fruit: the reprobate mind issuing in wickedness. The catalogue of sins displays the moral ruin flowing from the rejection of God. The verse displays the intensive force: filled, all, full. The verse displays the variety: from unrighteousness to whispering. The verse displays the principle: the abandoned mind producing every vice. The catalogue of sins flowing from the reprobate mind displays the moral ruin of the heathen world — saturated with unrighteousness, malice, envy, murder, and slander, the practical fruit of abandoning the knowledge of God. The verse displays the depth of corruption to which the judicial abandonment led, and continues the indictment (vv.30-31).
30:
"Backbiters, haters of God, despiteful, proud, boasters, inventors of evil things, disobedient to parents," The witnesses note the continuation of the catalogue of sins. The grammatical voices note the list. Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson note the vices continue: backbiters (open slanderers, as against whisperers, secret), haters of God, despiteful (insolent, injurious), proud, boasters, inventors of evil things, disobedient to parents — a comprehensive indictment of the heathen character.

On "haters of God," the witnesses note the gravity. Some render it "hated of God" (passive), but most take it as active — haters of God, the deepest root of the heathen's corruption, enmity against their Maker.

On "inventors of evil things... disobedient to parents," the witnesses note the depth and breadth. The catalogue descends to those who devise new forms of evil and who violate the most natural bond (honoring parents). Calvin notes each vice briefly defined. The verse displays the continued catalogue of the heathen's sins.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the catalogue continues — backbiters (open slanderers, against the secret whisperers), haters of God (active, enmity against their Maker, the deepest root), despiteful (insolent, injurious), proud, boasters, inventors of evil things (devising new wickedness), disobedient to parents (violating the most natural bond) — a comprehensive indictment of the heathen character (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson). The verse displays the continued catalogue. The verse marks the breadth: from slander to disobedience. The verse displays the gravity: haters of God. The verse displays the depth: inventors of evil. The verse displays the violation: disobedient to parents. The continued catalogue displays the comprehensive corruption of the heathen. The verse displays the root: enmity against God. The verse displays the invention: devising new evils. The verse displays the unnatural: dishonoring parents. The continued catalogue of the heathen's sins — backbiters, haters of God, proud, inventors of evil, disobedient to parents — displays the comprehensive corruption flowing from the abandoned mind, descending even to enmity against God and the violation of the most natural human bonds. The verse displays the depth and breadth of the moral ruin Paul charges against the Gentile world, completing the indictment (v.31). The verse displays the thoroughness of the corruption that follows the rejection of God.
31:
"Without understanding, covenantbreakers, without natural affection, implacable, unmerciful:" The witnesses note the close of the catalogue of sins. The grammatical voices note the final list. Barnes, Ellicott, and Robertson note the closing vices: without understanding (senseless, void of moral discernment), covenantbreakers (faithless, breaking compacts), without natural affection (lacking even the love of kindred), implacable (unforgiving — though the best manuscripts may omit this, Ellicott), unmerciful (without pity).

On "without natural affection," the witnesses note the depth. This vice — lacking the natural love of parents for children and children for parents — marks the extreme of corruption, the breaking even of nature's bonds.

The witnesses note the climax of the indictment: the heathen sunk so low as to lose even natural affection and mercy. Calvin notes the vices show the abandoned mind void of all judgment. The verse displays the close of the catalogue of the heathen's sins.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the catalogue closes with — without understanding (senseless, void of moral discernment), covenantbreakers (faithless), without natural affection (lacking even the love of kindred, the extreme of corruption), implacable (unforgiving, though perhaps omitted in the best manuscripts, Ellicott), unmerciful (without pity) — the heathen sunk so low as to lose even nature's bonds (Barnes, Ellicott, Robertson). Calvin notes the vices show the abandoned mind void of all judgment. The verse displays the close of the catalogue. The verse marks the depth: without natural affection. The verse displays the climax: losing even natural love and mercy. The verse displays the senselessness: without understanding. The verse displays the faithlessness: covenantbreakers. The close of the catalogue displays the extreme of the heathen's corruption. The verse displays the breaking of nature's bonds. The verse displays the loss of mercy and affection. The verse displays the void of judgment in the abandoned mind. The close of the catalogue of the heathen's sins — without understanding, covenantbreakers, without natural affection, unmerciful — displays the extreme depth of the moral ruin, the heathen sunk so low as to lose even the natural affections and mercy that belong to humanity itself. The verse displays the terminus of the judicial abandonment: a corruption so deep it breaks even the bonds of nature. The verse sets up the climactic charge (v.32).
32:
"Who knowing the judgment of God, that they which commit such things are worthy of death, not only do the same, but have pleasure in them that do them." The witnesses note the climactic charge: pleasure in others' sins. On "knowing the judgment of God," the grammatical voices note the aggravation. Barnes and Robertson note the heathen knew (by conscience, natural law) that such deeds deserved death (God's righteous judgment) — their sin was against knowledge, leaving them inexcusable.

On "worthy of death," the witnesses note the desert. The natural conscience testified that such sins merited God's condemnation — death, the just penalty.

On "not only do the same, but have pleasure in them that do them," the witnesses note the climax. Calvin, Barnes, and Robertson note this is the height of wickedness: not merely committing sin (which passion might excuse), but approving and delighting in others' sins — a settled, deliberate approval of evil, the deepest depravity. To take pleasure in others' sin is worse than to sin oneself, for it shows a mind wholly given to evil. The verse displays the climactic charge: knowing approval of sin.

The witnesses agree: the heathen knew the judgment of God — that such deeds deserved death (by conscience and natural law, their sin against knowledge, inexcusable, Barnes, Robertson) — yet not only did them but had pleasure in those who did them: the climax of wickedness (Calvin, Barnes, Robertson). To approve and delight in others' sin is worse than to sin oneself, for passion might excuse the act, but pleasure in evil shows a mind wholly given to it, a settled, deliberate approval — the deepest depravity. The verse displays the climactic charge: knowing approval of sin. The verse marks the aggravation: sin against knowledge. The verse displays the desert: worthy of death. The verse displays the climax: pleasure in others' sin. The verse displays the depth: deliberate approval of evil. The climactic charge displays the terminus of the heathen's depravity: not ignorance but knowing approval of sin. The verse displays the inexcusableness: they knew the judgment. The verse displays the worst degree: delighting in evil. The verse displays the settled wickedness: approving what God condemns. The climactic charge — knowing God's judgment, yet not only committing sin but taking pleasure in those who do it — displays the deepest depth of the heathen's depravity: a settled, deliberate approval of evil against the testimony of conscience, worse than the sinful act itself. The verse closes the indictment of the Gentile world, proving all under sin and inexcusable before God, and prepares for the turn to the Jew (ch. 2) and the universal need of the righteousness of God by faith. The verse displays the culmination of Paul's argument that the heathen, knowing God's judgment, are wholly without excuse.
